text: The surgeon performs extensive adhesiolysis using monopolar scissors and a bipolar sealing device to free the liver from the diaphragm. Segment IVb is particularly challenging to free due to its location at the previous transection plane from the right hepatectomy. The liver hilum is also cleared of adhesions to facilitate the Pringle maneuver.

The surgeon performs the Pringle maneuver using the Hwang-Loop technique, placing a Foley catheter around the hepatoduodenal ligament for inflow control. An intraoperative ultrasound is conducted to accurately identify the tumor within the liver.

The surgeon marks the transection line on the liver surface using monopolar scissors. After demarcating the lesion, a traction suture is placed using a resorbable multifilament suture and secured with a hemo-lock.
The surgeon applies traction on the Foley catheter to perform the Pringle maneuver, securing it with a bulldog clamp for effective inflow control.

The surgeon progressively resects the liver lesion using a bipolar sealing device for sealing and cutting, as well as caliclamping to identify portal pedicles. Vessels and bile ducts are clipped with hemo-locks and transected using the bipolar sealing device. Parenchymal transection continues until the tumor lesion is fully resected.

Gauzes are placed on the transected liver parenchyma, with gentle pressure applied and a sprinkle of water to assess for bleeding and bile leaks. The gauze is left in place for a minute for observation.

The specimen is placed in an amber catch bag within the abdominal cavity. The gauze is removed from the transected liver surface, revealing a spot of bile. The surgeon inspects the liver surface and identifies the site of bile leak, which is then controlled using a barred monofilament suture.

The surgeon releases the Pringle maneuver by removing the bulldog clamp and loosening the Foley catheter. Hemostasis is checked, and Surgiflo hemostatic matrix is applied to areas as needed. After confirming no residual bleeding, the Foley catheter is removed.

The surgeon applies a fibrillar absorbable hemostat to the surgical site to ensure hemostasis. Attention then shifts to repairing the incisional hernia, where a primary closure is performed using a barbed monofilament resorbable suture, avoiding mesh placement due to the recent liver surgery.
At the conclusion of the hepatectomy, a silicone drain is placed in the surgical site due to a perioperative bile leak. The specimen is exteriorized by enlarging the incision.
